title: Your Return Expectation Is Older Than You Think
author: Bhartendra Singh
body_html:
Every time the market goes sideways for long enough, the same sentence starts circulating. The India story is finished, and whatever worked for the last twenty years has quietly stopped working.

Count how often this has happened. Sanctions after Pokhran and an Asian currency crisis arrived in the same year. Russia defaulted the year after that, then the internet bubble collapsed. In 2008, oil at $145, a rupee down a quarter, attacks in Mumbai, and a governance fraud at Satyam three months later. The Fragile Five in 2013. IL&FS and a frozen credit market in 2018, with COVID waiting behind it. A war, American rates at six percent, and $129 oil in 2022. Six separate occasions in under thirty years when informed opinion held that the country had run out of road.

All six now read as buying opportunities, though not one of them felt that way to anybody living inside it.

The unfinished one is the only one that frightens you

A horror film you have already seen is a different object from the one you are watching for the first time. The footage has not changed and neither has the jump at the forty minute mark. What changed is that you know the ending, and knowing the ending converts fear into pattern.

Every crisis on that list has an ending attached to it now. The one you happen to be standing inside has not been assigned an ending yet, and will not be for years. So the mind quietly reclassifies 2008 as a lesson while treating the present as a threat to survival, and the gap between those two classifications has nothing to do with severity.

This is why the sixth crisis always feels like the first. Investors are not being stupid about it. Memory does something to a completed event that it simply cannot do to one still running.

What actually happens in a flat market

Take a stretch where the index goes nowhere, which registers to the person holding it as loss. Look at what is happening underneath and the arithmetic tells a different story.

Nominal GDP keeps compounding, averaging around twelve percent a year over three decades and dipping to roughly ten in the weak patches. Earnings recover from a bad quarter and climb back to high single digits. Corporate profit as a share of GDP, once below two percent, moves up toward five. Price stands still while the earnings underneath it grow, which means valuation corrects on its own, quietly, without a single dramatic red day. Add currency depreciation and the country gets meaningfully cheaper in dollar terms while the headlines describe a collapse.

The gap between how loud a period sounds and how much it actually moved is where most investor damage happens. A flat stretch following a strong one is closer to weather than to punishment.

The number in your head is from another country

Most investors have never audited the number itself.

The Indian equity investor carries a return expectation of fifteen to twenty percent, and that number is real. Investors earned every point of it. It was also produced by a macro regime that no longer exists.

Work backwards from the risk free rate. When equity was delivering fifteen to twenty, the government borrowed at nine percent. Inflation sat near seven and nominal GDP was growing at thirteen to fourteen. Stack an equity risk premium on a nine percent base and fifteen falls out as arithmetic, with no magic anywhere in it.

The government now borrows at six to seven and inflation has come down alongside it. India's equity risk premium has compressed, on Damodaran's own published series, because the country is measurably less risky than it used to be. A lower base carries a smaller premium on top of it, and what arrives at the end is a smaller nominal return. Twelve to thirteen percent today sits on that new foundation the way sixteen to seventeen sat on the old one, which makes the achievement underneath identical even though the printed number has shrunk.

India got safer, and a maturing market pays less for the same work. That is the entire explanation for the smaller number.

What a stale expectation costs you

An outdated number would be harmless if it only produced disappointment. It does something considerably more expensive than that.

If you believe you are owed seventeen percent and your portfolio delivers twelve, you go looking for the missing five. There is always somebody selling it. When the bank pays six and an instrument promises sixteen, that ten point gap is a description of risk that nobody has read out loud to you. The biggest blunders in investing get made by people reaching for high yield in a low yield world, and the reach usually starts with a sense of entitlement to a number that expired years ago.

So take the correction to your yardstick before you take it to your portfolio. Recalibrate the expectation and twelve percent stops registering as underperformance. The hunt for the missing five loses its reason to exist, and a flat stretch goes back to being ordinary.

Do that once and most of the noise loses its grip on you. The market will keep producing sixth crises for as long as you are invested in it. Your job is only to notice, while it is happening, that you are describing your mood and calling it analysis.
excerpt: Every time the market goes sideways for long enough, the same sentence starts circulating. The India story is finished, and whatever worked for the last twenty years has quietly stopped working.

title: Why Your SIP Feels Like It's Doing Nothing
slug: Why Your SIP Feels Like It's Doing Nothing
author: Bhartendra Singh
body_html:
Every time the market goes sideways for a while, the same doubts come back. That SIPs don't make money. That you should keep your money moving instead of parking it. That India's story is finally cracking, the foreign money is leaving, and the smart returns are somewhere else. It always sounds reasonable, and it always spreads just as people begin cancelling their SIPs, close to half of them within thirty months of starting.

Thirty years of data disagrees with the entire list. A ₹10,000 monthly SIP held across three decades turned about ₹36 lakh of contributions into ₹18 to ₹22 crore. Six or seven funds did it, through every crisis and every correction, including the stretches when the news said to get out. The real question underneath the noise is simple: can you sit still long enough to let one of them work?

The people who lose money are rarely in bad funds. They are in good funds and cannot stay put. Investing gets sold as an information problem: pick the right fund, read the right returns, time the right entry. The bigger variable is how long you hold.

### Volatility is the bill

Markets consolidate and correct. After a long run-up, a flat or falling stretch is normal, the shape every equity market has held for fifty years. Each time, we assemble a fresh reason why this fall is the dangerous one: the war, the oil spike, the weak rupee, the FIIs leaving. Underneath all of it, the rupee has lost about 3.5% a year on average since 1947, through every one of those stories. Volatility is the price you are paid to accept. A market that never fell would be a market that never rewarded you for staying.

### The one sale you run from

Put 30% off on shirts and the whole city runs to the store. A correction is 30% off on equity, and the same people cancel their SIP. A fall is a discount on units. Rupee cost averaging works because you buy more when the price drops, so the fall is the engine, and quitting during it is how you stall the engine. The maths rewards whoever keeps going, and we mistake the quitting for caution.

### The silent decade

Compounding does its early work in the dark. For eight or nine years an equity SIP looks unremarkable, and the portfolio gives you nothing to boast about. Then the curve turns and the number jumps. Almost nobody waits for it. Close to half of all SIPs are cancelled within two and a half years, and the average holding period in India sits near three and a half. The wealth sits on the far side of a decade most people quit, and most of the damage happens inside that same decade. A flat stretch that makes people panic is just this decade doing its ordinary quiet work.

### You already know how to do this

Indian households already hold assets for twenty years without blinking. We do it with gold and with property. Nobody sells the family flat over two weak quarters, and nobody dumps gold in a soft year; they wait, or they buy more, because they believe the price comes back. Equity is the only one of the three we bail on, and it is the only one of the three that clears inflation. The patience already exists in the house. It just never reaches the equity folder.

### The mistake is usually yours

The fund manager is rarely the problem. When a fund disappoints, the buyer usually entered at the wrong time and judged it against the wrong benchmark. The top fund this year will not be the top fund next year, and that turnover is how the game works. The costlier habit is chasing returns, which is exactly what the noise always encourages: move your money, jump to whatever just ran up, keep circulating. Hopping between funds and hunting whoever topped the one-year chart has cost ordinary portfolios the plain market return they would have earned by sitting still. Chase the extra and you tend to lose the ordinary.

The real variable is the expectation you carry in. Expect 12% and 12% keeps you invested, with the money still doubling roughly every six years. Expect 25% and the first 12% year sends you looking elsewhere, usually right before the decade pays.

### What to actually do

Audit your own behaviour before you audit the fund. Set the expectation at 12% and let compounding stay boring. Treat a crash like a festival sale and keep the SIP running through it. Fund retirement before every other goal, because a house, a wedding, and a degree all come with a loan, and old age does not. And because money turns everyone emotional, keep one steady person beside you for twenty years whose only task is to stop you selling at the wrong moment. That role earns more than any fund they could pick.

The doubt comes back in a different costume every few years, and the data has answered it every time. The returns belong to whoever can sit through the boredom of the silent decade and let it finish.
excerpt: Every time the market goes sideways for a while, the same doubts come back. That SIPs don't make money. That you should keep your money moving instead of parking it. That India's story is finally crac…
